title: Chorizo and Butter Bean Stew
subtitle: Spanish chorizo with butter beans in smoked tomato broth
excerpt: A deeply savoury stew of cooking chorizo, butter beans, smoked paprika, and tomato, simmered until the beans absorb the spiced oil and the broth turns rust-red. Ready in 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Spanish bean and chorizo stews appear across the country under various names — fabada, potaje, cocido. This streamlined version using tinned butter beans reflects everyday home cooking. Documented in Penelope Casas, The Foods and Wines of Spain (1982) and Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The beans will break down slightly on reheating, which thickens the broth.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily with the same cooking time.

content.text: The chorizo does most of the work here. As it fries, the orange-red fat renders and flavours the oil; everything else cooked in that fat picks up the paprika and pork depth without any additional spicing. The butter beans absorb it all — they should not be crisp or firm by the end, but yielding, almost creamy, fully saturated with the broth.
Serve with bread. The broth is the point.

items.prepNote: taste before adding — the chorizo is salty

content.text: Fry the chorizo and build the base

Heat the olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the chorizo and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until browned and the red-orange fat has rendered into the pan. Add the onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and smoked paprika, stir for 1 minute. Add the tinned tomatoes and cook for 5 minutes, breaking up the tomatoes with a spoon.

Add the butter beans, stock, and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 to 20 minutes until the broth has thickened slightly and the beans are fully tender. Crush a few beans against the side of the pan to help thicken the broth. Remove the bay leaves, season, and scatter with parsley. Serve with crusty bread.

items.cause: Not enough simmering time or beans not crushed
items.fix: Crush more beans against the pan wall; simmer uncovered for a further 10 minutes

items.cause: Chorizo is already quite salty; combined with stock the salt builds
items.fix: Use low-sodium stock and don't add salt until you have tasted the finished dish

content.text: Spanish bean stews are winter food and weekend food — something that simmers on the back of the hob while the household does other things. The famous Asturian fabada uses fabes (large local white beans) and morcilla as well as chorizo, but this version, made with tinned butter beans and a single cut of chorizo, gives almost the same result in a fraction of the time. The key is the smoked paprika: it carries the whole flavour of the dish.
